s: The moonlit mosque that had to face the street sideways
w: al-Aqmar means moonlit, and its carved stone facade glows accordingly. Commissioned in 1125 by the Fatimid vizier al-Mamun — arrested the very year it was finished, executed three years later. No minaret: too close to the caliph's palace for anyone to look down into it.
_facts: Built 1125 by Fatimid vizier al-Ma'mun al-Bata'ihi, who was arrested that same year and executed three years later
Name al-Aqmar means 'moonlit' in Arabic
Located on al-Mu'izz Street, on what was once the ceremonial avenue flanked by Fatimid caliphal palaces
First mosque in Cairo to have its facade angled relative to its interior to align with the street grid while maintaining correct qibla orientation
No minaret constructed: proximity to the caliph's palace meant no one could climb and look down inside

s: Built for a caliph who vanished without a trace in 1021
w: Started in 990, finished in 1013; then its patron rode into the Muqattam Hills in 1021 and vanished. al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah was murdered, translated to heaven, or simply lost, depending on the sect. The mosque rotted for centuries; the Dawoodi Bohra community rebuilt it stone by stone in 1980.
_facts: Construction begun 990 by Caliph al-Aziz, completed 1013 by al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah
al-Hakim disappeared in February 1021, cause unknown — theories include assassination, divine translation (Druze belief), or simple murder
Mosque was neglected for centuries, used as a stable, a prison, and a warehouse at various points
Major restoration and reconstruction completed 1980 by the Dawoodi Bohra Ismaili community, allowing it to reopen for worship
Located just south of Bab al-Futuh, on the east side of al-Mu'izz Street

s: Built on a prison, minarets perched on Bab Zuwayla towers
w: al-Mu'ayyad Shaykh had been imprisoned in a cell next to Bab Zuwayla under a previous sultan. When he took the throne, he razed the prison and built this mosque in its place, 1415-1421. His flourish: both minarets rise from the tops of Bab Zuwayla's own Fatimid towers, a trick no other Cairo mosque has managed.
_facts: Complex built 1415-1421 by Mamluk sultan al-Mu'ayyad Shaykh
Built on the site of a prison next to Bab Zuwayla where al-Mu'ayyad had been imprisoned under a previous sultan
The mosque's two minarets are uniquely built on top of the older Fatimid-era towers of Bab Zuwayla — a feature unique to this mosque
Complex includes a Friday mosque, two mausoleums, and originally a madrasa
Contains the tomb of Sultan al-Mu'ayyad and family members

s: Fake-medieval Neo-Mamluk, the last Shah of Iran is buried here
w: Looks 700 years old; construction ran 1869-1912. Built as a Neo-Mamluk companion to Sultan Hassan opposite, it holds the royal mausoleum of Egypt's Muhammad Ali dynasty — and one uninvited guest: Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, the last Shah of Iran, who died in Cairo in 1980 and was laid here when no one else would take him.
_facts: Construction ran 1869-1912, commissioned originally by Hoshiyar Qadin, mother of Khedive Ismail
Neo-Mamluk style, architecturally conceived as companion to the Sultan Hassan Mosque opposite
Contains royal mausoleum of Egypt's Muhammad Ali dynasty: Khedive Ismail, King Fuad I, King Farouk among those interred
Mohammad Reza Pahlavi (last Shah of Iran) died in Cairo July 1980 after the Iranian Revolution and was buried here
Architect Max Herz, Austro-Hungarian, supervised completion phase 1905-1912

s: That clock came from Paris — in exchange for a Luxor obelisk
w: The clock tower arrived in 1846: Louis Philippe of France sent it to Muhammad Ali Pasha in exchange for the Luxor obelisk now standing at the centre of Place de la Concorde in Paris. The clock has rarely worked since. Ottoman domes above have overlooked Cairo from the Citadel since 1857.
_facts: Commissioned by Muhammad Ali Pasha, built 1832-1857
Clock tower in the courtyard was a gift from French King Louis Philippe in c.1846, given in exchange for the Luxor obelisk now at Place de la Concorde in Paris
Clock has reportedly rarely functioned; restored under King Farouk's reign
Built in Ottoman style with European influences, unlike earlier Cairene architecture — sometimes called the Alabaster Mosque
Muhammad Ali Pasha is buried here in a tomb of Carrara marble, transferred in 1857

s: Largest freestanding tomb in Egypt, deep in the City of the Dead
w: al-Shafi'i founded one of Islam's four Sunni schools of jurisprudence; he died here in 819. Saladin built the first mausoleum in 1176. Sultan al-Kamil raised the dome in 1211 — the largest wooden dome in Egypt — atop what remains the country's largest freestanding tomb. Deep in the City of the Dead.
_facts: Imam al-Shafi'i died in Cairo 819 CE; founder of the Shafi'i school of Sunni Islamic jurisprudence
Saladin built the first turbah and madrasa here in 1176 as part of Ayyubid effort to consolidate Sunnism
Sultan al-Kamil raised the current mausoleum dome in 1211 — one of the largest domes of its time, only smaller than the Dome of the Rock
Considered the largest freestanding tomb in Egypt
Mamluk Sultan Qaytbay added renovations to the dome and mihrabs in 1480

s: Open every hour since 1797, Naguib Mahfouz never left
w: Open every hour of every day since 1797, run by the same family for at least seven generations. Naguib Mahfouz came so often his corner table is effectively consecrated. Mirrors, brass lanterns, backgammon, street spilling in from all sides. The Egyptian government recognises it as one of the country's oldest cafes.
_facts: Opened in Khan el-Khalili market in 1797, making it one of the oldest cafes in Egypt
Run by successive generations of the same family for at least seven generations
Nobel laureate Naguib Mahfouz was a regular, as was chemist Ahmed Zewail
Open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week continuously since founding
Recognised by the Egyptian government as a historic institution

s: The last great caravanserai of medieval Cairo, still standing
w: Built in 1504-05 by Sultan Qansuh al-Ghuri: merchants stored goods below, slept above, traded in the courtyard; low-income tenants occupied the upper floors. The best-preserved caravanserai in Cairo. On Thursday evenings the courtyard fills with Sufi whirling and folk music rather than spice merchants.
_facts: Built 1504-05 by Sultan al-Ashraf Qansuh al-Ghuri as part of a larger religious-civic complex
A wikala (caravanserai/commercial agency) housed merchants and their goods; upper floors were a rab' — low-income residential apartments
Revenues were waqf (endowment) to fund the adjacent religious complex (khanqah, sabil-kuttab, mosque-madrasa)
Considered one of the most impressive and best-preserved examples of this building type in Cairo
Now an arts center hosting folk music and Sufi whirling performances

s: 100,000 objects, car-bombed in 2014, rebuilt and open again
w: Over 100,000 objects from seven centuries of Islamic craft: Quran manuscripts in silver ink, carved wooden mashrabiyya screens, Mamluk metalwork. The 1902 neo-Mamluk building was badly damaged in January 2014 when a car bomb across the street erased its carved facade. Painstakingly restored; reopened 2017.
_facts: Houses over 100,000 objects though only about 4,500 displayed; collection spans 7th-19th centuries from across the Islamic world
Current building designed by Alfonso Manescalo, completed 1902 in neo-Mamluk style
Car bomb targeting the police headquarters opposite on 24 January 2014 severely damaged the building and destroyed many artifacts
Restoration cost estimated at US$10 million; museum reopened January 2017
Collection includes rare Quran manuscripts with silver ink calligraphy

s: World's largest Coptic collection, facade copied from al-Aqmar Mosque
w: Egypt's Christian centuries — between pharaoh and Islam — compressed into one 1908 mansion in Old Cairo: icons, textiles, carved ivory, Nag Hammadi texts. The world's largest Coptic collection, its facade modelled on al-Aqmar Mosque. Six ancient churches share the grounds, several dating to the fifth century.
_facts: Founded 1908 by Marcus Simaika Pasha, inaugurated 14 March 1910
Largest collection of Coptic Christian artifacts in the world
Facade of the museum modelled on the facade of the 12th-century al-Aqmar Mosque
Became a state museum in 1931; 1939 the Christian antiquities collection from the Egyptian Museum was moved here
Surrounded by six ancient churches including the Hanging Church and the Church of St Sergius

s: Napoleon's scholars wrote the Description de l'Egypte here
w: Built in 1794 by an official from Sennar, Sudan. In 1798 Napoleon's scholars commandeered it: Gaspard Monge presided, Fourier was secretary, and the Description de l'Egypte was planned here. The alley outside is still called Monge Passage. Now a cultural centre managed by the Bibliotheca Alexandrina.
_facts: Built in 1794 by Ibrahim Katkhuda al-Sinnari, whose surname refers to the city of Sennar
In 1798 confiscated by the French to house the Committee of Sciences and Arts accompanying Napoleon; the Description de l'Egypte was planned here
Gaspard Monge was president, Napoleon deputy, Joseph Fourier secretary of the institute
The alley giving access to the house is named Monge Passage after the mathematician
Currently managed by the Bibliotheca Alexandrina as a cultural center after earthquake restoration in the 1990s

s: Cairo's first octagonal minaret, built by the sultan's son-in-law
w: al-Maridani started as the sultan's cupbearer at 16, became his son-in-law. In 1340 he built the most extravagant mosque in Cairo: re-used Roman and Pharaonic columns lining the portico, and Egypt's first fully octagonal minaret. Tucked into the Darb al-Ahmar lanes, usually quiet.
_facts: Built 1340 CE by Amir Altinbugha al-Maridani, who rose from cupbearer to son-in-law of Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad
At the time of construction considered one of the most extravagantly decorated mosques in Cairo
Featured the first fully octagonal minaret in Egypt
Re-used ancient columns from Pharaonic and Roman sites in the mosque portico
Located in the Darb al-Ahmar neighbourhood, south of Bab Zuwayla

s: Cairo's only surviving covered market street, sewn in applique cloth
w: Cairo's only surviving covered market street, built around 1650 by Radwan Bey, who controlled the Mecca pilgrimage for 25 consecutive years. The stalls still sell khayamiya: the bold applique textile stitched for funeral tents and Ramadan celebrations. Shafts of light fall through the vaulted wooden roof.
_facts: Built c.1650 CE by Radwan Bey, a Mamluk Bey who held the post of amir al-hajj (organiser of the Mecca pilgrimage) for 25 consecutive years
Only existing example of a historic covered market street in Cairo
Also known as al-Khayamiya and the Street of the Tentmakers
Last major market dedicated to the sale of khayamiya — decorative applique textiles used for funeral tents and Ramadan celebrations
Located directly south of Bab Zuwayla gate, outside the historic walled city

s: The palace that replaced the Citadel, now oddly open to visitors
w: Khedive Ismail finished this in 1874 to replace the Citadel as Egypt's seat of government. It remained the royal household's official address until the 1952 revolution; now a presidential palace with a public museum wing: ceremonial weapons, silver plate, and the clocks that kept time for a dynasty.
_facts: Built by Khedive Ismail, completed 1874 as the new Egyptian royal household's primary official residence
Replaced the Citadel of Cairo as the center of Egyptian government
Served as official residence until the 1952 Egyptian Revolution led by Gamal Abdel Nasser
Has been a presidential palace since 1952 but a portion is open as a museum
Museum includes collections of ceremonial weapons, silver, and historical clocks

s: 1087 Fatimid gate, Mamluk gravestones mortared into the ramparts above
w: Gate of Victory, 1087, built by the Fatimid vizier Badr al-Jamali — with Armenian stonemasons, hence the cruciform arrow slits and cut-stone construction. Climb the hollow towers and emerge above the medieval street. The Mamluk gravestones mortared into the outer ramparts came from the cemetery directly outside.
_facts: Built 1087, ordered by Fatimid vizier Badr al-Jamali
One of three remaining gates of historic Cairo's city wall (the others: Bab al-Futuh to the west, Bab Zuwayla to the south)
Unusual cut-stone construction (rather than brick) with cruciform arrow slits, attributed to Armenian stonemasons imported for the project
The towers are hollow and can be climbed to emerge above the medieval street
Located at the northern end of Shari'a al-Gamaliya, slightly east of Bab al-Futuh

s: 970 CE and still running: the university that predates Oxford by a century
w: Founded in 970 CE as the first mosque of the new Fatimid capital. Its university began teaching in 989 — older than Bologna, older than Oxford. Closed by Saladin after the Fatimid fall, restored under the Mamluks: a thousand years of Islamic Egypt in a single courtyard, still packed with students at noon.
_facts: Founded 970 CE by Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli, first mosque of the new Fatimid capital
Al-Azhar University began teaching in 989, making it the second oldest continuously operating university in the world (after Al-Qarawiyyin in Fes)
Shunned by Saladin and the Ayyubid dynasty after the fall of the Fatimid Caliphate; status as congregational mosque removed
Restored under the Mamluk Sultanate with numerous expansions
Nationalized and designated an independent university in 1961 following the 1952 Egyptian Revolution
